quote: it's a single family home. Is there anybody else is there anybody else on the title but you?
stewart_read: Rep correctly identified the wife as a co-title holder at 01:15. The protocol failure is what happened next: the wife was never mentioned again. The appointment was booked for Chris alone, with no attempt to include her. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to make the appointment the venue for spouse inclusion — 'What time works for both of you?' The rep asked the right question and then ignored the answer.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep may have assessed Chris as the primary decision-maker and planned to let the closer handle spouse coordination. Rejected — there is no evidence of this intent, and the schema is explicit that setters who 'win' this objection lose the deal.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. Rep asked the title question correctly — that's the foundation. The gap is the follow-through. Walk through the protocols.spouse_decision template: 'This is the kind of decision both of you should be part of — what time works for both of you?' The appointment is structurally vulnerable until the wife is on it.

quote: Oh, so we look at your power bill. We remove it, and we replace it with a lower cheaper solar bill. If you can't save you money in the first year, we're not allowed to offer anything at all. And then that's how you pay that lower cheaper monthly bill than what you're paying now. And then the panels and the installation, there's no upfront cost.
stewart_read: The customer asked an open question ('tell me how this works') — a gift. The rep responded with a generic feature-list explanation that never once referenced the customer's own bill ($150/month, stated at 01:36). The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' The rep had the bill number in hand and did not use it. A suggested rephrase (not verbatim): 'You mentioned you're paying around $150 a month — what we do is look at exactly that number and replace it with a lower solar payment. If we can't beat what you're paying Dominion, we don't offer you anything.' The customer's own number is the anchor; the generic explanation is a missed opportunity.
coaching_implication: This is the bill_anchoring gap the schema flags as universal across the corpus. Rep had the bill number, had the customer's attention, and had a direct question — three conditions that rarely align this cleanly. Use this clip to demonstrate the difference between 'explaining solar' and 'anchoring to their pain.' The flip was available and unused.

quote: How much are you spending on a monthly average?
stewart_read: The customer was never asked what interested him in solar. The rep moved directly from address/title/utility to bill amount, skipping the interest_in_solar verify item entirely. The schema marks this as 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' with depth_grading:true — it is the item most likely to surface the customer's real motivation (bill pain, neighbor influence, environmental concern) and the one that gives the rep language to use throughout the rest of the call. Its absence means the rep had no emotional hook to tie back to during the pitch or the button_up. The prior_designs item was also never asked, leaving competitive context unknown.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep may have judged the customer as already-qualified and skipped the emotional anchor to move efficiently to booking. Rejected — the customer's proactive question at 02:29 ('tell me how this works') signals he had motivation worth surfacing. The skip cost the rep the language he needed for the bill-anchor moment.
coaching_implication: Two missing verify items: interest_in_solar and prior_designs. The interest_in_solar gap is the higher-priority coaching beat — it is the emotional anchor the entire pitch should hang on. Ask the rep: 'What do you think Chris's reason for filling out the form was?' The honest answer is 'I don't know' — and that's the coaching point. The prior_designs gap is secondary but matters for the closer's competitive context.

quote: Okay. Perfect. And then I can schedule you right after you send that, and we'll be good to go.
stewart_read: Rep made the appointment contingent on bill receipt. The bill was apparently sent and received during the call (rep confirms at 05:34: 'Yeah. That's perfect'), so the contingency resolved on-call. However, the sequencing created an unnecessary risk window: if the customer had not sent the bill, no appointment would have been booked. The stronger move is to lock the appointment first and treat the bill as a parallel task — 'Let me get you on the calendar right now, and while I'm doing that, go ahead and send that over.' The bill is a data requirement; the appointment is the primary objective.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep may have used the bill-send as a commitment device — getting the customer to take an action before booking increases psychological investment. Possible, but there is no evidence this was intentional, and the risk of the contingency not resolving outweighs the commitment-device benefit on a warm customer who was already cooperative.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the sequencing risk: 'What happens if he doesn't send the bill?' On a warm customer this worked out. On a colder customer, the contingency becomes an exit ramp. Teach the parallel-task framing: book first, collect bill simultaneously.

quote: So we'll call you Monday at 5PM. Okay? And then I'll text you after this to remind you.
stewart_read: The button_up was materially incomplete. Of the six required elements in button_up.required_elements, the rep completed two: appointment datetime confirmed and text follow-up promise. Missing: one_hour_block_stated, no_driving_at_computer, and is_that_fair_handshake. The 'is that fair?' verbal handshake is schema-flagged as predictive of show rate. The no-driving/at-computer anchor is flagged as directly correlated with no-show rate. The rep also called the customer 'Mike' at 06:26 ('Okay. Thanks, Mike') — the customer's name is Chris. This is a hygiene gap, not a rapport repair, as no correction occurred in the transcript.
coaching_implication: Walk through the button_up checklist with the rep against this call. He hit 2 of 6. The missing items are not optional — the schema flags no_driving_at_computer and is_that_fair_handshake as directly tied to show rate. Also flag the name error: calling a customer by the wrong name at close is a trust erosion that the rep may not have noticed. Quick fix: confirm the name before the close line.

stewart_read: Rep closed the call by addressing the customer as 'Mike.' The customer's name is Chris (confirmed at 00:31: 'Hey. Is this Chris?'). No correction occurred. This is a hygiene gap — the rep either lost track of the name during the call or confused this customer with another. On a warm customer who was already committed, the damage is likely minimal. On a colder customer, a wrong-name close signals inattention and can erode the trust built during the call.
coaching_implication: Low-stakes coaching moment but worth a quick mention: name discipline at close. Suggest the rep confirm the name before the close line as a habit — 'Alright Chris, we're all set.' Takes two seconds and eliminates the risk.
